content.problemStatement:
Serega is an avid traveller. He has already visited numerous exotic places, and now wants to embark on a journey to Treeland. There are **N** cities in Treeland that Serega wants to visit. Also, roads have been laid between some **N-1** pairs of cities in Treeland. Roads are bidirectional, and there exists a unique path between any two Treeland cities.


Each of Treeland's roads has its own class, wich is specified using a single digit. Serega's journey is a path between some cities, i.e. he never visits one city more than once. But Serega wants to select a path such that the sequence of classes on the path is a palindrome. Of course, Serega wants to visit as many cities as possible. Please help Him.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases in a single file will not exceed 3*10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of cities in Treeland. The next N-1 lines contain three integers v, u, and c, representing a road of class c between the vth and uth cities of Treeland.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the maximum number of cities that Serega can visit.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The sequence of classes on the path between the cities 5 and 8 is 210012.
The sequence of classes on the path between the cities 1 and 3 is 121.
The sequence of classes on the path between the cities 1 and 2 is 12.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a depth-first search (DFS) approach. We start by choosing an arbitrary node as the root of the tree. Then, for each node, we perform a DFS to find the longest palindromic path that starts and ends at that node. We keep track of the maximum length of such a path found so far. The key observation here is that a palindromic path can be formed by concatenating two identical paths in reverse order. Therefore, we can use a recursive DFS function to explore all possible paths and keep track of the maximum length of palindromic paths found.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N^2) — where T is the number of test cases and N is the number of cities in Treeland. The reason for this time complexity is that in the worst case, we need to perform a DFS from each node, and each DFS can take up to O(N) time.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — where N is the number of cities in Treeland. This is because we need to store the adjacency list representation of the tree, which can take up to O(N) space.
